content:
5 min read
Just now

--

I remember the date I wrote my first original song: February 4th, 2000. The song has been lost to time, but it sparked an outpouring of music from my adolescent brain. Of all the songs I wrote in high school, I only remembered the chorus from one of them, but at least it was the best of the bunch. Moving onto college I was much more prolific, though erratic in my writing. I’d spend three montsh furiously writing music, then take seven months off or so with maybe a song here and there. The dream was set: I wanted to be a rock star.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Diane Picchiottino on UnsplashBetween college and my early adulthood I struggled to find a band. My voice was average at best and the same could be said for my guitar skills. I could never get over that hump into elite playing, no matter how often I practiced. I can’t snap my fingers, so figured I had something physically wrong with my hands that prevented me from getting over that hump. I felt like Shaquille O’Neal. No matter how many free throws he took, he never improved his percentage.
A friend suggested I move to bass instead of guitar (insert joke here), but again, I had that limited ceiling. Despite all my physical and vocal deficiencies, I still believed in my songwriting. I just needed to find a band or artist I connected with, we’d write songs together, and we’d be huge. I’d be “Guitar George,” knowing all the chords, but unable to make it cry or sing. I could play background guitar or maybe bass, but my main contribution would be the writing.
As I’m sure you assumed, it never happened. I moved to Los Angeles and would regularly play bars and coffee shops for a while. I’d connect with people through Craigslist and our styles wouldn’t fit, or my physical talent would itself as a deterrent. I’d still write songs for girls I dated or a hook would come to my mind and I’d put it to paper, but aside from a private performance or an occasional party, nobody would ever hear it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by John on UnsplashFast forward to July of 2026. I see an ad for Suno, an AI music generator, and I decide to check it out. Signing up for the free trial, I put in a demo for a song I recorded years earlier and am stunned with the results. I had to make some tweaks, but wow was it incredible to hear the song I’ve had in my head for decades finally come to life before my ears. This was what I was going for my entire life.
I was obsessed. I signed up for Suno’s Pro program, which allows you to own and license your own music. I put over forty of my songs through their system and even generated a few fun songs for my kids. After signing up through DistroKid’s distribution program, I released two albums and as of the time of this article, planned a third for release next week. The lyrics are 100% written by myself. The chords and melodies are all mine, but the performance is all AI.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Vitaly Gariev on UnsplashIt’s with that demarcation that I still feel good about it as an artist. I didn’t take hilarious texts or Tinder conversations and have them generated into a song. I didn’t write something like “Make me a hip-hop song about baked potatoes” or whatever and sit back while it generated some garbage. I took my own songs, one of which was twenty-six years old, and brought them to life. The lyrics, the hooks, the melodies were all mine. Obviously, Suno adds some flourishes and makes some tweaks, but is it any different from what a human producer would do had I been given the chance? What is the difference between myself and some songwriter behind the scenes other than I wasn’t lucky enough to get a record deal? Talent only takes you so far sometimes.
So, I released most of my music July 17, 2026 and didn’t market other than sending it to my friends. Practically all of them have been impressed. I chose the band name “The Scours” because it was my 20th choice for a name that wasn’t taken and I remembered thinking I was “scouring” the internet for a name. Standout tracks included “Let Me Down,” “I Wanna Be Your Bed,” and “Born in a Bordello.” For all of these songs I have the lyrics from a word document created in the early 2000’s and my original recordings that I put into the Suno system. “Wanna Be Your Bed” is my “newest” song at about ten years old. I am forty-two years old and I can finally hear my life’s work come to fruition. We’re in the midst of a heat wave and I’ve been driving down the road with my windows down, blasting my own songs like I’m twenty-one years old again.
Naturally, there’s a stigma against AI music. I get that. I’ve written seven books to this point and can’t stand the idea of authors using AI to pump out “literature.” My books are self-published and I do it all: writing, editing, blurb, etc., except the covers, which I always hire a human being to create (I’ve never been artistic in that manner. My hands, remember?). Again, I’m in my forties with a family. I barely play my instruments anymore. My rock star days have passed me by. There was no avenue for me to get my songs out in any other manner. So, here we are.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by William Bout on UnsplashI’m proud of what I’ve written and I think my songs are very good. They deserve to be listened to, and in a world with so many one-hit wonders I believe my material is at least better than, well, practically all of them. Some of mine are better than other, sure, but that’s any band. The Beatles are the greatest band of all time, but come on, they have a few stinkers. My music deserves to be heard, and if this is the only way to do it, so be it.
If you don’t like it, feel free to listen to the Scours on Spotify, Apple Music, or the dozen of other platforms it’s available on and if you like the tune but hate the AI, you have my permission to cover the song.
As long as I get my 10%.
